intro.description: A review checkpoint. A passage may contain anywhere from zero to several fallacies — read the whole thing, then mark only what is genuinely faulty.

sentences.text: Last spring the city ran a six-month pilot of the new bus route in four neighborhoods.
Ridership was logged automatically by the fare readers on every vehicle.
The transit office compared the pilot corridors against three similar routes left unchanged.
Weather and fuel prices were recorded so they could be accounted for later.
Residents could submit comments through a short weekly form.
Average commute times in the pilot corridors fell by about four minutes.
The analysts noted that a mild winter may have made part of that gain easier.
Satisfaction scores rose modestly, though the response rate was uneven.
Two of the four neighborhoods showed almost no change at all.
The report lists these limits plainly rather than glossing over them.
Given the mixed picture, the board proposes extending the pilot for another year.
A larger sample should make the seasonal effects easier to separate.
The office will publish the raw ridership data alongside its next summary.
If the extended trial shows no benefit, the route will return to its old form.
No permanent change is recommended until the follow-up is complete.

hints.text: Ask whether any claim outruns its evidence — careful reasoning is not a fallacy.
Notice the limits, comparisons, and follow-up. Look for overreach you won't find here.
This brief reasons carefully and contains no fallacy. The right move may be to mark nothing.

sentences.text: The new bus route has quickly become a point of pride for the city.
Supporters point to shorter waits and fuller buses during the morning rush.
Anyone who still opposes the route is just a selfish driver who ignores their neighbors.
The transit office has shared its ridership figures for anyone to review.
Local businesses have offered mixed but mostly positive feedback.
Critics raise fair questions about parking near the busiest stops.
Either we expand the route to every district now, or the whole system will fall apart.
City planners say a phased rollout is one option under discussion.
The budget for the next phase has not yet been finalized.
A public comment period is scheduled before any vote.
Supporters argue the early results are encouraging, if still preliminary.
We must approve the expansion immediately, before our children inherit a gridlocked, dying city.
Opponents counter that more data would strengthen the case either way.
The council has asked for a follow-up study before deciding.
Both sides agree the next report should settle several open questions.

expected.rationale: It dismisses opponents by calling them selfish rather than answering their reasons.
It offers only 'expand everywhere now' or 'collapse,' ignoring the phased option the very next sentence mentions.
It pushes immediate approval with a frightening image instead of evidence.

hints.text: Watch for a Personal Attack, a Distorted Choice, and an Emotional push — about one per paragraph.
Most sentences report facts or open questions; three do persuasive work the evidence should.
'selfish driver' = Ad Hominem; 'expand now or it all falls apart' = False Dilemma; 'gridlocked, dying city' = Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: The debate over the riverside development has grown heated this season.
One developer says a friend's project across the state succeeded, so this one surely will too.
City records show the proposal is still under environmental review.
Most of the loudest voices online already back the plan, so it must be the right call.
The planning office has posted the full application for public viewing.
Opponents have asked for a traffic study before any approval.
So the opponents apparently want the waterfront left to rot forever.
The developer notes that permits in the next county were granted quickly.
Ever since the rumor spread, nearby home prices ticked up, so the project is clearly already helping.
Appraisers caution that many factors move home prices at once.
Either the council approves the full plan tonight or the investment leaves for good.
Staff say a smaller first phase remains on the table.
A neutral consultant has been hired to review the figures.
The council will hold one more hearing next week.
Both sides expect the consultant's report to shape the final vote.

expected.rationale: A single project elsewhere is offered as proof this one will succeed.
Online popularity is treated as proof the plan is right.
A request for a traffic study is distorted into wanting the waterfront to rot.
A price uptick after a rumor is treated as proof the project caused it.
It offers only 'approve tonight' or 'lose the investment,' ignoring the smaller phase named next.

hints.text: Look for Bad Evidence, Framing by popularity, a Distorted argument, a Causation slip, and a Distorted choice.
Five sentences are faulty; the rest report records, cautions, or next steps.
friend's project = Anecdotal; 'loudest voices online' = Bandwagon; 'left to rot' = Straw Man; 'prices ticked up, so helping' = Post Hoc; 'approve tonight or it leaves' = False Dilemma.
